attributes.stem:
A 63-year-old man is brought to the general practice clinic by his son after being found wandering around the shopping mall. His son mentioned that his father is becoming more forgetful over the past month, and has difficulty with simple daily task such as buttoning his shirt. His son also reports a couple of episodes where his father would get irritated easily by his neighbour. At times, he would shout aggressively towards his family during their visits. On the most recent visit, his son noticed several instances where his father would stare blankly unresponsively during conversations. The patient claims he sees children running around his garden. He has a medical history of COPD, hyperlipidaemia, and was diagnosed with Parkinson’s Disease 10 months ago. His current medications include levodopa, salbutamol, and rosuvastatin.

On examination, his vital signs are stable. He appears confused and oriented to place and person but not to time. There is a resting tremor in his left upper limb. He is able to walk independently without assistance. However, he has slow gait with short steps.

He scored 14/30 on the Mini mental status examination (MMSE).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient’s condition?
attributes.explanation:
Lewy body dementia presents with visual hallucination, early onset of postural instability and autonomic nervous system involvement. Poor response to levodopa is also a common feature of this condition.

It is typical for Lewy body dementia to present with symptoms of parkinsonism, however the main difference between diagnosis of Parkinson and Lewy body dementia is the onset of cognitive impairment in relation to motor impairments. Patients with Lewy body dementia presents with cognitive impairment within one year of onset of parkinsonism. Cognitive impairment in Parkinson typically presents much later, around 10 years after motor and gait impairments. Furthermore, In Lewy body dementia, dementia commonly occurs earlier (about aged 50 years) and is more severe in relation to the motor and gait disturbances. Other characteristic features of Lewy body dementia include poor sleep-wake patterns.

In this case, it is likely that the patient was thought to have Parkinson disease due to his motor impairment, however the new onset of hallucinations in less than a year makes the diagnosis of Lewy body dementia much more likely.

Vascular dementia may present with dementia and loss of cognitive function. However, this pattern of onset is typically abrupt with a stepwise deterioration. Vascular dementia rarely cause visual hallucination.

Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) is caused by temporal and frontal lobe atrophy. This manifest as early changes in behaviour and personality i.e. irritability and loss of social cues. Dementia and parkinsonism findings are typically late stage signs in this disease. Frontotemporal dementia also occurs in the mid-40s to late 60’s age range. Some types of FTD can have impairment in word comprehension and speech production. From cognitive perspective, can have difficulties with executive functioning and working memory.

Normal pressure hydrocephalus presents as dementia, gait abnormalities and urinary incontinence. However, urinary incontinence is not seen in this patient and visual hallucinations are not typically seen in normal pressure hydrocephalus.

Lastly, Creutz-Jakob disease may occur due to underlying prion disease, and manifest as visual hallucinations and dementia. However, dementia progression is usually rapid and there would be other findings such as myoclonus and seizures.
learningPoints: Lewy Body Dementia typically has parkinsonism and dementia onset within one year
references: Knopman D, Boeve B, Petersen R. Essentials of the Proper Diagnoses of Mild Cognitive Impairment, Dementia, and Major Subtypes of Dementia. Mayo Clinic Proceedings. 2003;78(10):1290-1308.
McKeith I, Boeve B, Dickson D, Halliday G, Taylor J, Weintraub D et al. Diagnosis and management of dementia with Lewy bodies. Neurology. 2017;89(1):88-100.
